Researchers found that M-Powering Teachers improves instructors’ uptake of student contributions by 13% and present suggestive evidence that it also improves students’ satisfaction with the course and assignment completion.

Yip, Elizabeth Bonsignore, Daniel Pauw, Caroline Pitt American Educational Research Journal, April 2023 Researchers examined a sociotechnical system called Science Everywhere, which invited the broader community to interact with science learning experiences youths shared across home, school, and community settings.

Buttimer, Joshua Littenberg-Tobias, Justin Reich AERA Open, January 2022 Researchers found that participants changed their equity mind-sets and self-reported equity practices, and that these changes in attitudes and behaviors persisted—and in some cases increased—on the 4-month follow-up survey.

Rafalow, Cassidy Puckett Educational Researcher, January 2022 Researchers show how educational institutions have long played a role in constructing the value of technologies to different ends, by constructing hierarchies of technological activity, like “vocational” and “academic” computer use, even when strikingly similar.

relations.source: Disrupting Educational Inequalities Through Youth Digital Activism
relations.sentence: Disrupting Educational Inequalities Through Youth Digital Activism Amy Stornaiuolo, Ebony Elizabeth Thomas Review of Research in Education, June 2017 Researchers examine young people’s use of online tools for organizing toward social change across three lines of inquiry—young people’s cultural and political uses of participatory tools and spaces online, new forms of youth civic engagement and activism, and adult-supported programs and spaces facilitating youth activism.

relations.source: Improving Access to, Quality, and the Effectiveness of Digital Tutoring
relations.sentence: Improving Access to, Quality, and the Effectiveness of Digital Tutoring in K–12 Education Patricia Burch, Annalee Good, Carolyn Heinrich Educational Evaluation and Policy Analysis, 2016 Researchers found significant associations between formats, curriculum drivers, tutor locations, and other characteristics of digital providers and their effectiveness in increasing student achievement, as well as differential access by student characteristics, that warrant further investigation as digital providers’ roles in K–12 instruction continue to expand.

relations.source: Going the Distance With Online Education
relations.sentence: Going the Distance With Online Education Jorge Larreamendy-Joerns, Gaea Leinhardt Review of Educational Research, Winter 2006 The article reviews the history of distance education through the lenses of three historical themes—democratization, liberal education, and educational quality—and charts the current scene of online education in terms of three educational visions that may inform the development of online initiatives: the presentational view, the performance-tutoring view, and the epistemic-engagement view.

verb_relations.sentence: Teachers’ love for teaching, empathy for students, and readiness to guide them ahead can all help to build great ties and communication between them and their students.

verb_relations.subject: Teachers
verb_relations.sentence: Teachers must be flexible, devoted, and timely in responding to messages and emails, providing comments, and grading tasks because of the typical characteristics of online education.

verb_relations.subject: teachers
verb_relations.sentence: Good online teachers encourage students to share their opinions and experiences and support group projects by using effective technology and online discussion boards.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: Teachers that are well-organized can accomplish these objectives by providing students with all course materials and tools, as well as training them on how to use these resources effectively.

verb_relations.subject: obstacles
verb_relations.sentence:
Four research questions will be addressed based on the research’s main objectives:
Research question 1: What are teachers’ perceptions and attitudes towards implementing online educations in primary and high schools?
Research question 2: What are the obstacles to applying the English language’s online teaching from the teachers’ perspectives?

verb_relations.subject: role
verb_relations.sentence: Research question 3: What is the role of teachers’ demographic background in their willingness to implement E-learning and online?

verb_relations.subject: study
verb_relations.sentence: In addition to the research questions stated, this study explores eight hypotheses based on survey data concerned with teachers of the English language.

verb_relations.subject: hypotheses
verb_relations.sentence: These hypotheses offer new insights on the fundamental arguments of the study.

verb_relations.subject: Teachers
verb_relations.sentence: Hypothesis 3: Teachers of various ages have diverse perspectives on fully implementing the English language’s online teaching.

verb_relations.subject: Teachers
verb_relations.sentence: Hypothesis 4: Teachers of different ages have different perceptions of partially implementing the English language’s online teaching.

verb_relations.subject: Teachers
verb_relations.sentence: Hypothesis 5: Teachers with different job locations (cities, large towns, small towns, and villages) have different perceptions of fully implementing online teaching of the English language.

verb_relations.subject: it
verb_relations.sentence: In other words, it is expected that urban teachers will demonstrate more willingness to use online teaching compared to their counterparts in villages because online education is better linked with the advancements and contemporary culture of cities than it is with the villages.

verb_relations.subject: Teachers
verb_relations.sentence: Hypothesis 6: Teachers with different job locations (cities, large towns, small towns, and villages) have different perceptions of implementing partially online English language teaching.

verb_relations.subject: hierarchy
verb_relations.sentence: Hypothesis 7: It is predicted that the settlement hierarchy will have a significant impact on teachers’ perceptions of fully implementing online teaching.

verb_relations.subject: residences
verb_relations.sentence: In other words, different residences of teachers (cities, large towns, small towns, and villages) influence teachers’ perceptions of fully implementing online teaching of the English language.

verb_relations.subject: residences
verb_relations.sentence: Hypothesis 8: Different residences of teachers (cities, large towns, small towns, and villages) influence teachers’ perceptions of partially implementing online teaching of the English language.

verb_relations.subject: hypotheses
verb_relations.sentence: Notes: Hypotheses 5 and 6 concern the size of teachers’ job location while hypotheses 7 and 8 concern the geographical residence of teachers.

verb_relations.subject: study
verb_relations.sentence:
Methodology
This study used an online questionnaire created with ‘Google Forms’ (N = 968) as a survey method with English teachers’ responses in primary and high schools.

Results & discussion
The following analysis has been broken down into sections that correlate to the hypotheses and questions of the research.

verb_relations.subject: which
verb_relations.sentence:
Introducing online education from the teachers’ standpoint
This part delves into the first research question, which analyzes teachers’ attitudes toward executing online teaching of the English language across primary schools and high schools.

Table 2 indicates that most respondents, or 85.1%, disapproved of completely applying online English language teaching.completely applying online English language teaching; only 11.3% were in favor of saying yes while 3.6% selected did not know.

verb_relations.subject: respondents
verb_relations.sentence: In the next section, the respondents expressed their concerns about online teaching by determining several factors.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: Regarding the partial implementation of online teaching, Table 2 reveals that nearly half of respondents (48.2%) approve of partially incorporating online teaching, while 45.5% oppose it, and 6.5% are undecided (they said they didn’t know), indicating that they are unsure about whether or not to support online teaching.

verb_relations.subject: project
verb_relations.sentence: It comes as no surprise that they neither agree nor disagree with online teaching because the Ministry of Education has not invested enough time and energy in making an online teaching project successful and has not paid any attention to teacher training about online teaching.

verb_relations.subject: teaching

verb_relations.subject: obstacles

verb_relations.subject: preponderance
verb_relations.sentence: Essentially, the data reveals that launching online education is currently unachievable since the preponderance of teachers and students lack ICT skills and the required communication tools, especially computers and cellphones with reliable internet service.

verb_relations.subject: which
verb_relations.sentence: However, these issues can be resolved if the government will take these obstacles into serious consideration, which needs subsidies and training before introducing online teaching.

verb_relations.sentence: Unless online teaching is practiced within schools, mistrust represents one of the biggest obstacles, as 43.4% of respondents believe that students do not trust online teaching, and 23.5% of them stated the Ministry of Education doubted teachers’ competencies in implementing online teaching.

verb_relations.sentence: This indicates that teachers’ geographical residence does not matter because the majority of teachers (53.6%) lack the experience of using ICT in teaching (Table 4).

verb_relations.sentence: Several studies suggest that e-learning can empower and enable developing nations overcome educational obstacles and create new experiences by applying the web and visual technologies to promote social and professional interaction among learners whenever and wherever they need it.(Drigas & Tsolaki, 2015).

verb_relations.sentence: E-learning can mitigate the shortcomings of traditional education techniques by allowing instructors to impart material to a large number of students despite time, space, and facility constraints.

This research has revealed that 85.1% of respondents did not support fully implementing online teaching of the English language, but 48.2% of the respondents favored partially implementing online teaching for several reasons.

relations.sentence: To address these issues, this paper (1) briefly defines AI through the concepts of machine learning and algorithms; (2) introduces applications of AI in educational settings and benefits of AI systems to support students’ learning processes; (3) describes ethical challenges and dilemmas of using AI in education; and (4) addresses the teaching and understanding of AI by providing recommended instructional resources from two providers—i.e., the Massachusetts Institute of Technology’s (MIT) Media Lab and Code.org.

To address these issues, this paper (1) briefly defines AI through the concepts of machine learning and algorithms; (2) introduces applications of AI in educational settings and benefits of AI systems to support students’ learning processes; (3) describes ethical challenges and dilemmas of using AI in education; and (4) addresses the teaching and understanding of AI by providing recommended instructional resources from two providers—i.e., the Massachusetts Institute of Technology’s (MIT) Media Lab and Code.org.

Therefore, this article: (a) synthesizes ethical issues surrounding AI in education as identified in the educational literature, (b) reflects on different approaches and curriculum materials available for teaching students about AI and ethics (i.e., featuring materials from the MIT Media Lab and Code.org), and (c) articulates future directions for research and recommendations for practitioners seeking to navigate AI and ethics in K-12 settings.

For example, the European Union (EU) has highlighted ethical concerns and implications of AI, emphasized privacy protection, surveillance, and non-discrimination as primary areas of interest, and provided guidelines on how trustworthy AI should be [3, 15, 23].

"AI and Ethics Curriulum" for middle school students by MIT Media Lab
The MIT Media Lab team offers an open-access curriculum on AI and ethics for middle school students and teachers.

“AI and Data Privacy” workshop series for K-9 students by MIT Media Lab
Another quality resource from the MIT Media Lab’s Personal Robots Group is a workshop series designed to teach students (between the ages 7 and 14) about data privacy and introduce them to designing and prototyping data privacy features.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence:
Abstract
Artificial intelligence (AI) is a field of study that combines the applications of machine learning, algorithm productions, and natural language processing.

verb_relations.subject: Applications
verb_relations.sentence: Applications of AI transform the tools of education.

verb_relations.subject: AI
verb_relations.sentence: AI has a variety of educational applications, such as personalized learning platforms to promote students’ learning, automated assessment systems to aid teachers, and facial recognition systems to generate insights about learners’ behaviors.

verb_relations.subject: paper
verb_relations.sentence: To address these issues, this paper (1) briefly defines AI through the concepts of machine learning and algorithms; (2) introduces applications of AI in educational settings and benefits of AI systems to support students’ learning processes; (3) describes ethical challenges and dilemmas of using AI in education; and (4) addresses the teaching and understanding of AI by providing recommended instructional resources from two providers—i.e., the Massachusetts Institute of Technology’s (MIT) Media Lab and Code.org.

verb_relations.subject: teachers
verb_relations.sentence: The article aims to help practitioners reap the benefits and navigate ethical challenges of integrating AI in K-12 classrooms, while also introducing instructional resources that teachers can use to advance K-12 students’ understanding of AI and ethics.

verb_relations.subject: Success
verb_relations.sentence: “Success in creating AI would be the biggest event in human history.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: Unfortunately, it might also be the last, unless we learn how to avoid the risks.”—Stephen Hawking.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: We may not think about artificial intelligence (AI) on a daily basis, but it is all around us, and we have been using it for years.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: When we are doing a Google search, reading our emails, getting a doctor’s appointment, asking for driving directions, or getting movie and music recommendations, we are constantly using the applications of AI and its assistance in our lives.

verb_relations.subject: impact
verb_relations.sentence: The growing impact and dominance of AI systems reveals itself in healthcare, education, communications, transportation, agriculture, and more.

verb_relations.subject: It
verb_relations.sentence: It is almost impossible to live in a modern society without encountering applications powered by AI [10, 32].

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: Artificial intelligence (AI) can be defined briefly as the branch of computer science that deals with the simulation of intelligent behavior in computers and their capacity to mimic, and ideally improve, human behavior [43].

verb_relations.subject: AI
verb_relations.sentence: AI dominates the fields of science, engineering, and technology, but also is present in education through machine-learning systems and algorithm productions [43].

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: For instance, AI has a variety of algorithmic applications in education, such as personalized learning systems to promote students’ learning, automated assessment systems to support teachers in evaluating what students know, and facial recognition systems to provide insights about learners’ behaviors [49].

verb_relations.subject: which
verb_relations.sentence: Here, we use the American term “K–12 education” to refer to students’ education in kindergarten (K) (ages 5–6) through 12th grade (ages 17–18) in the United States, which is similar to primary and secondary education or pre-college level schooling in other countries.

verb_relations.subject: systems
verb_relations.sentence: These AI systems can increase the capacity of K-12 educational systems and support the social and cognitive development of students and teachers [55, 8].

verb_relations.subject: systems
verb_relations.sentence: More specifically, applications of AI can support instruction in mixed-ability classrooms; while personalized learning systems provide students with detailed and timely feedback about their writing products, automated assessment systems support teachers by freeing them from excessive workloads [26, 42].

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: Despite the benefits of AI applications for education, they pose societal and ethical drawbacks.

verb_relations.subject: scientist
verb_relations.sentence: As the famous scientist, Stephen Hawking, pointed out that weighing these risks is vital for the future of humanity.

verb_relations.subject: it
verb_relations.sentence: Therefore, it is critical to take action toward addressing them.

verb_relations.subject: risks
verb_relations.sentence: The biggest risks of integrating these algorithms in K-12 contexts are: (a) perpetuating existing systemic bias and discrimination, (b) perpetuating unfairness for students from mostly disadvantaged and marginalized groups, and (c) amplifying racism, sexism, xenophobia, and other forms of injustice and inequity [40].

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: As academics, scientists, and citizens, we have a responsibility to educate teachers and students to recognize the ethical challenges and implications of algorithm use.

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: To create a future generation where an inclusive and diverse citizenry can participate in the development of the future of AI, we need to develop opportunities for K-12 students and teachers to learn about AI via AI- and ethics-based curricula and professional development [2, 58

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: Toward this end, the existing literature provides little guidance and contains a limited number of studies that focus on supporting K-12 students and teachers’ understanding of social, cultural, and ethical implications of AI [2].

verb_relations.subject: few
verb_relations.sentence: Most studies reflect university students’ engagement with ethical ideas about algorithmic bias, but few addresses how to promote students’ understanding of AI and ethics in K-12 settings.

verb_relations.subject: article
verb_relations.sentence: Therefore, this article: (a) synthesizes ethical issues surrounding AI in education as identified in the educational literature, (b) reflects on different approaches and curriculum materials available for teaching students about AI and ethics (i.e., featuring materials from the MIT Media Lab and Code.org), and (c) articulates future directions for research and recommendations for practitioners seeking to navigate AI and ethics in K-12 settings.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: Next, we briefly define the notion of artificial intelligence (AI) and its applications through machine-learning and algorithm systems.

verb_relations.subject: discussion
verb_relations.sentence: As educational and educational technology scholars working in the United States, and at the risk of oversimplifying, we provide only a brief definition of AI below, and recognize that definitions of AI are complex, multidimensional, and contested in the literature [9, 16, 38]; an in-depth discussion of these complexities, however, is beyond the scope of this paper.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: Second, we describe in more detail five applications of AI in education, outlining their potential benefits for educators and students.

verb_relations.subject: algorithms
verb_relations.sentence: Third, we describe the ethical challenges they raise by posing the question: “how and in what ways do algorithms manipulate us?”

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: Fourth, we explain how to support students’ learning about AI and ethics through different curriculum materials and teaching practices in K-12 settings.

verb_relations.subject: practitioners
verb_relations.sentence: Our goal here is to provide strategies for practitioners to reap the benefits while navigating the ethical challenges.

verb_relations.subject: educators
verb_relations.sentence: We acknowledge that in centering this work within U.S. education, we highlight certain ethical issues that educators in other parts of the world may see as less prominent.

verb_relations.subject: AI
verb_relations.sentence: For example, the European Union (EU) has highlighted ethical concerns and implications of AI, emphasized privacy protection, surveillance, and non-discrimination as primary areas of interest, and provided guidelines on how trustworthy AI should be [3, 15, 23].

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: Finally, we reflect on future directions for educational and other research that could support K-12 teachers and students in reaping the benefits while mitigating the drawbacks of AI in education.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence:
Definition and applications of artificial intelligence
The pursuit of creating intelligent machines that replicate human behavior has accelerated with the realization of artificial intelligence.

verb_relations.subject: definition
verb_relations.sentence: This particular definition highlights the mimicry of human behavior and consciousness.

verb_relations.subject: definition
verb_relations.sentence: This definition incorporates the different sub-fields of AI together and underlines their function while reaching at or above human capability.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: Combining these definitions, artificial intelligence can be described as the technology that builds systems to think and act like humans with the ability of achieving goals.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: An algorithm is a set of rules or instructions that is to be followed by computers in problem-solving operations to achieve an intended end goal.

verb_relations.subject: computer
verb_relations.sentence: They involve thousands of lines of codes which represent mathematical instructions that the computer follows to solve the intended problems (e.g., as computing numerical calculation, processing an image, and grammar-checking in an essay).

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: In that way, algorithms can provide instructions for almost any AI system and application we can conceive [27].

verb_relations.subject: computers
verb_relations.sentence: Machine learning is about making computers act or perform without being given any line-by-line step [29].

verb_relations.subject: algorithms
verb_relations.sentence: Machine-learning algorithms first analyze the data to determine patterns and to build a model and then predict future values through these models.

verb_relations.subject: it
verb_relations.sentence: First, it analyzes and gathers the data, and then, it builds a model to excel for different tasks, and finally, it undertakes the action and produces the desired results successfully without human intervention [29, 56].

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: These applications of AI have shown promise to support teachers and students in various ways: (a) providing instruction in mixed-ability classrooms, (b) providing students with detailed and timely feedback on their writing products, (c) freeing teachers from the burden of possessing all knowledge and giving them more room to support their students while they are observing, discussing, and gathering information in their collaborative knowledge-building processes [26, 50].

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: Below, we outline benefits of each of these educational applications in the K-12 setting before turning to a synthesis of their ethical challenges and drawbacks.

verb_relations.subject: systems
verb_relations.sentence: Personalized learning systems, also known as adaptive learning platforms or intelligent tutoring systems, are one of the most common and valuable applications of AI to support students and teachers.

verb_relations.subject: They
verb_relations.sentence: They provide students access to different learning materials based on their individual learning needs and subjects [55].

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: For example, rather than practicing chemistry on a worksheet or reading a textbook, students may use an adaptive and interactive multimedia version of the course content [39].

verb_relations.subject: instruction
verb_relations.sentence: Comparing students’ scores on researcher-developed or standardized tests, research shows that the instruction based on personalized learning systems resulted in higher test scores than traditional teacher-led instruction [36].

verb_relations.subject: AI
verb_relations.sentence: Microsoft’s recent report (2018) of over 2000 students and teachers from Singapore, the U.S., the UK, and Canada shows that AI supports students’ learning progressions.

verb_relations.subject: platforms
verb_relations.sentence: These platforms promise to identify gaps in students’ prior knowledge by accommodating learning tools and materials to support students’ growth.

verb_relations.subject: platforms
verb_relations.sentence: These systems generate models of learners using their knowledge and cognition; however, the existing platforms do not yet provide models for learners’ social, emotional, and motivational states [28].

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: Considering the shift to remote K-12 education during the COVID-19 pandemic, personalized learning systems offer a promising form of distance learning that could reshape K-12 instruction for the future [35].

verb_relations.subject: algorithms
verb_relations.sentence: Assessment algorithms can provide course support and management tools to lessen teachers’ workload, as well as extend their capacity and productivity.

verb_relations.subject: systems
verb_relations.sentence: Ideally, these systems can provide levels of support to students, as their essays can be graded quickly [55].

verb_relations.subject: Providers
verb_relations.sentence: Providers of the biggest open online courses such as Coursera and EdX have integrated automated scoring engines into their learning platforms to assess the writings of hundreds of students [42].

verb_relations.subject: tool
verb_relations.sentence: By flagging the wrong answers and marking the correct ones, the tool supports instructors by eliminating their manual grading time and effort.

verb_relations.subject: which
verb_relations.sentence: Thus, automated assessment systems deal very differently with marking and giving feedback to essays compared to numeric assessments which analyze right or wrong answers on the test.

verb_relations.subject: systems
verb_relations.sentence: Overall, these scoring systems have the potential to deal with the complexities of the teaching context and support students’ learning process by providing them with feedback and guidance to improve and revise their writing.

verb_relations.subject: teachers
verb_relations.sentence: These systems provide insights about students’ behaviors during learning processes and allow teachers to take action or intervene, which, in turn, helps teachers develop learner-centered practices and increase student’s engagement [55].

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: For example, these analytics can be used to detect university students who are at risk of failing or not completing a course.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: Through these identifications, instructors can intervene and get students the help they need [55].

verb_relations.subject: sites
verb_relations.sentence:
Social networking sites and chatbots
Social networking sites (SNSs) connect students and teachers through social media outlets.

verb_relations.subject: Researchers
verb_relations.sentence: Researchers have emphasized the importance of using SNSs (such as Facebook) to expand learning opportunities beyond the classroom, monitor students’ well-being, and deepen student–teacher relations [5].

verb_relations.subject: scholars
verb_relations.sentence: Different scholars have examined the role of social media in education, describing its impact on student and teacher learning and scholarly communication [6].

verb_relations.subject: integration
verb_relations.sentence: They point out that the integration of social media can foster students’ active learning, collaboration skills, and connections with communities beyond the classroom [6].

verb_relations.subject: Chatbots
verb_relations.sentence: Chatbots also take place in social media outlets through different AI systems [21].

verb_relations.subject: them
verb_relations.sentence: In summary, applications of AI can positively impact students’ and teachers’ educational experiences and help them address instructional challenges and concerns.

verb_relations.subject: Students
verb_relations.sentence: Students have a wide range of learning styles and needs.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: Therefore, it is critical for teachers and students to understand the limits, potential risks, and ethical drawbacks of AI applications in education if they are to reap the benefits of AI and minimize the costs [11].

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence:
Ethical concerns and potential risks of AI applications in education
The ethical challenges and risks posed by AI systems seemingly run counter to marketing efforts that present algorithms to the public as if they are objective and value-neutral tools.

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: In essence, algorithms reflect the values of their builders who hold positions of power [26].

verb_relations.subject: which
verb_relations.sentence: Whenever people create algorithms, they also create a set of data that represent society’s historical and systemic biases, which ultimately transform into algorithmic bias.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: Even though the bias is embedded into the algorithmic model with no explicit intention, we can see various gender and racial biases in different AI-based platforms [54].

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: Considering the different forms of bias and ethical challenges of AI applications in K-12 settings, we will focus on problems of privacy, surveillance, autonomy, bias, and discrimination (see Fig. 1).

verb_relations.subject: educators
verb_relations.sentence: However, it is important to acknowledge that educators will have different ethical concerns and challenges depending on their students’ grade and age of development.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: Where strategies and resources are recommended, we indicate the age and/or grade level of student(s) they are targeting (Fig. 2).

verb_relations.subject: One
verb_relations.sentence: One of the biggest ethical issues surrounding the use of AI in K-12 education relates to the privacy concerns of students and teachers [47, 49, 54].

verb_relations.subject: people
verb_relations.sentence: Privacy violations mainly occur as people expose an excessive amount of personal information in online platforms.

verb_relations.subject: violations
verb_relations.sentence: Although existing legislation and standards exist to protect sensitive personal data, AI-based tech companies’ violations with respect to data access and security increase people’s privacy concerns [42, 54].

verb_relations.subject: systems
verb_relations.sentence: To address these concerns, AI systems ask for users’ consent to access their personal data.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: Although consent requests are designed to be protective measures and to help alleviate privacy concerns, many individuals give their consent without knowing or considering the extent of the information (metadata) they are sharing, such as the language spoken, racial identity, biographical data, and location [49].

verb_relations.subject: sharing
verb_relations.sentence: Such uninformed sharing in effect undermines human agency and privacy.

verb_relations.subject: systems
verb_relations.sentence: In other words, people’s agency diminishes as AI systems reduce introspective and independent thought [55].

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: Relatedly, scholars have raised the ethical issue of forcing students and parents to use these algorithms as part of their education even if they explicitly agree to give up privacy [14, 48].

verb_relations.subject: They
verb_relations.sentence: They really have no choice if these systems are required by public schools.

verb_relations.subject: which
verb_relations.sentence: Another ethical concern surrounding the use of AI in K-12 education is surveillance or tracking systems which gather detailed information about the actions and preferences of students and teachers.

verb_relations.subject: systems
verb_relations.sentence: Through algorithms and machine-learning models, AI tracking systems not only necessitate monitoring of activities but also determine the future preferences and actions of their users [47].

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: For instance, research suggests that teachers who use social networking sites (SNSs) for pedagogical purposes encounter a number of problems, such as concerns in relation to boundaries of privacy, friendship authority, as well as responsibility and availability [5].

verb_relations.subject: which
verb_relations.sentence: While monitoring and patrolling students’ actions might be considered part of a teacher’s responsibility and a pedagogical tool to intervene in dangerous online cases (such as cyber-bullying or exposure to sexual content), such actions can also be seen as surveillance systems which are problematic in terms of threatening students’ privacy.

verb_relations.subject: them
verb_relations.sentence: Monitoring and tracking students’ online conversations and actions also may limit their participation in the learning event and make them feel unsafe to take ownership for their ideas.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: How can students feel secure and safe, if they know that AI systems are used for surveilling and policing their thoughts and actions?

verb_relations.subject: systems
verb_relations.sentence: Problems also emerge when surveillance systems trigger issues related to autonomy, more specifically, the person’s ability to act on her or his own interest and values.

verb_relations.subject: systems
verb_relations.sentence: Predictive systems which are powered by algorithms jeopardize students and teachers’ autonomy and their ability to govern their own life [46, 47].

verb_relations.subject: Use
verb_relations.sentence: Use of algorithms to make predictions about individuals’ actions based on their information raise questions about fairness and self-freedom [19].

verb_relations.subject: bias
verb_relations.sentence:
Therefore, the risks of predictive analysis also include the perpetuation of existing bias and prejudices of social discrimination and stratification [42].
Finally, bias and discrimination are critical concerns in debates of AI ethics in K-12 education [6].

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: Gender bias is one of the most apparent forms of this problem, as the bias is revealed when students in language learning courses use AI to translate between a gender-specific language and one that is less-so.

verb_relations.subject: he
verb_relations.sentence: For example, while Google Translate translated the Turkish equivalent of “She/he is a nurse” into the feminine form, it also translated the Turkish equivalent of “She/he is a doctor” into the masculine form [33].

verb_relations.subject: models
verb_relations.sentence: This shows how AI models in language translation carry the societal biases and gender-specific stereotypes in the data [40].

verb_relations.subject: software
verb_relations.sentence: Research shows that facial recognition software has improperly misidentified a number of African American and Latino American people as convicted felons [42].

verb_relations.subject: algorithms
verb_relations.sentence: Additionally, biased decision-making algorithms reveal themselves throughout AI applications in K-12 education: personalized learning, automated assessment, SNSs, and predictive systems in education.

verb_relations.subject: incidents
verb_relations.sentence: Although the main promise of machine-learning models is increased accuracy and objectivity, current incidents have revealed the contrary.

verb_relations.subject: assessment
verb_relations.sentence: With the assessment of Ofqual’s algorithm based on schools' previous examination results, thousands of students were shocked to receive unexpectedly low grades.

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: Although a full discussion of the incident is beyond the scope of this article [51] it revealed how the score distribution favored students who attended private or independent schools, while students from underrepresented groups were hit hardest.

verb_relations.subject: concerns
verb_relations.sentence:
Unfortunately, automated assessment algorithms have the potential to reconstruct unfair and inconsistent results by disrupting student’s final scores and future careers [53].
Teaching and understanding AI and ethics in educational settings
These ethical concerns suggest an urgent need to introduce students and teachers to the ethical challenges surrounding AI applications in K-12 education and how to navigate them.

verb_relations.subject: groups
verb_relations.sentence: To meet this need, different research groups and nonprofit organizations offer a number of open-access resources based on AI and ethics.

verb_relations.subject: They
verb_relations.sentence: They provide instructional materials for students and teachers, such as lesson plans and hands-on activities, and professional learning materials for educators, such as open virtual learning sessions.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: Below, we describe and evaluate three resources: “AI and Ethics” curriculum and “AI and Data Privacy” workshop from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) Media Lab as well as Code.org’s “AI and Oceans” activity.

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: For readers who seek to investigate additional approaches and resources for K-12 level AI and ethics interaction, see: (a) The Chinese University of Hong Kong (CUHK)’s

verb_relations.subject: team
verb_relations.sentence:
"AI and Ethics Curriulum" for middle school students by MIT Media Lab
The MIT Media Lab team offers an open-access curriculum on AI and ethics for middle school students and teachers.

verb_relations.subject: curriculum
verb_relations.sentence: The curriculum includes various lessons tied to learning objectives.

verb_relations.subject: One
verb_relations.sentence: One of the main learning goals is to introduce students to basic components of AI through algorithms, datasets, and supervised machine-learning systems all while underlining the problem of algorithmic bias [45].

verb_relations.subject: it
verb_relations.sentence: In their AI Bingo chart, students try to identify what prediction the selected AI system makes and what dataset it uses.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: In the second investigation, “Algorithms as Opinions”, students think about algorithms as recipes, which are created by set of instructions that modify an input to produce an output [45].

verb_relations.subject: it
verb_relations.sentence: They explore what it means to be “best” and see how their opinions of best in their recipes are reflected in their algorithms.

verb_relations.subject: algorithms
verb_relations.sentence: In this way, students are able to figure out that algorithms can have various motives and goals.

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: Then, through their ethical matrix (chart), students identify different stakeholders (such as their parents, teacher, or doctor) who care about their peanut butter and jelly sandwich algorithm.

verb_relations.subject: values
verb_relations.sentence: Students fill out an ethical matrix and look for where those values conflict or overlap with each other.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: This matrix is a great tool for students to recognize different stakeholders in a system or society and how they are able to build and utilize the values of the algorithms in an ethical matrix.

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: Using Google’s Teachable Machine tool [2], students explore the supervised machine-learning systems.

verb_relations.subject: Students
verb_relations.sentence: Students train a cat–dog classifier using two different datasets.

verb_relations.subject: dataset
verb_relations.sentence: While the first dataset reflects the cats as the over-represented group, the second dataset indicates the equal and diverse representation between dogs and cats [2].

verb_relations.subject: dataset
verb_relations.sentence: Using these datasets, students compare the accuracy between the classifiers and then discuss which dataset and outcome are fairer.

verb_relations.subject: activity
verb_relations.sentence: This activity leads students into a discussion about the occurrence of bias in facial recognition algorithms and systems [2].

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: In the rest of the curriculum, similar to the AI Bingo investigation, students work with their partners to determine the various forms of AI systems in the YouTube platform (such as its recommender algorithm and advertisement matching algorithm).

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: Through the investigation of “YouTube Redesign”, students redesign YouTube’s recommender system.

verb_relations.subject: They
verb_relations.sentence: They first identify stakeholders and their values in the system, and then use an ethical matrix to reflect on the goals of their YouTube’s recommendation algorithm [45].

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: Finally, through the activity of “YouTube Socratic Seminar”, students read an abridged version of Wall Street Journal article by participating in a Socratic seminar.

verb_relations.subject: technologies
verb_relations.sentence: They discuss which stakeholders were most influential or significant in proposing changes in the YouTube Kids app and whether or not technologies like auto play should ever exist.

verb_relations.subject: you
verb_relations.sentence: During their discussion, students engage with the questions of: “Which stakeholder is making the most change or has the most power?”, “Have you ever seen an inappropriate piece of content on YouTube?

verb_relations.subject: you
verb_relations.sentence: What did you do?”

verb_relations.subject: teachers
verb_relations.sentence: Overall, the MIT Media Lab’s AI and Ethics curriculum is a high quality, open-access resource with which teachers can introduce middle school students to the risks and ethical implications of AI systems.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: The investigations described above involve students in collaborative, critical thinking activities that force them to wrestle with issues of bias and discrimination in AI, as well as surveillance and autonomy through the predictive systems and algorithmic bias.

verb_relations.subject: resource
verb_relations.sentence:
“AI and Data Privacy” workshop series for K-9 students by MIT Media Lab
Another quality resource from the MIT Media Lab’s Personal Robots Group is a workshop series designed to teach students (between the ages 7 and 14) about data privacy and introduce them to designing and prototyping data privacy features.

verb_relations.subject: group
verb_relations.sentence: The group has made the content, materials, worksheets, and activities of the workshop series into an open-access online document, freely available to teachers [44].

verb_relations.subject: privacy
verb_relations.sentence: During the workshop, students engage with the question of what privacy and data mean [44].

verb_relations.subject: They
verb_relations.sentence: They observe YouTube’s home page from the perspective of a mystery user.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: Using the clues from the videos, students make predictions about what the characters in the videos might look like or where they might live.

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: In a way, students imitate YouTube algorithms’ prediction mode about the characters.

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: Engaging with these questions and observations, students think further about why privacy and boundaries are important and how each algorithm will interpret us differently based on who creates the algorithm itself.

verb_relations.subject: workshop
verb_relations.sentence: The second workshop in the series is “Designing ads with transparency: A creative workshop”.

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: Through this workshop, students are able to think further about the meaning, aim, and impact of advertising and the role of advertisements in our lives [44].

verb_relations.subject: Students
verb_relations.sentence: Students collaboratively create an advertisement using an everyday object.

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: To do that, students learn about notions of malware and adware, as well as the components of YouTube advertisements (such as sponsored labels, logos, news sections, etc.).

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: By the end of the workshop, students design their ads as a poster, and they share with their peers.

verb_relations.subject: workshop
verb_relations.sentence: The final workshop in MIT’s AI and data privacy series is “Designing Privacy in Social Media Platforms”.

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: During the workshop, students create their own designs to solve one of the biggest challenges of the digital era: problems associated with online consent.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: The workshop allows students to learn more about the privacy laws and how they impact youth in terms of media consumption.

verb_relations.subject: Students
verb_relations.sentence: Students consider YouTube within the lenses of the Children’s Online Privacy Protections Rule (COPPA).

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: In this way, students reflect on one of the components of the legislation: how might students get parental permission (or verifiable consent)?

verb_relations.subject: resources
verb_relations.sentence: Such workshop resources seem promising in helping educate students and teachers about the ethical challenges of AI in education.

verb_relations.subject: resources
verb_relations.sentence: These workshop resources may facilitate teachers’ and students’ knowledge of data privacy issues and support them in thinking further about how to protect privacy online.

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: Moreover, educators seeking to implement such resources should consider engaging students in the larger question: who should own one’s data?

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: Teaching students the underlying reasons for laws and facilitating debate on the extent to which they are just or not could help get at this question.

verb_relations.subject: resource
verb_relations.sentence:
Investigation of “AI for Oceans” by Code.org
A third recommended resource for K-12 educators trying to navigate the ethical challenges of AI with their students comes from Code.org, a nonprofit organization focused on expanding students’ participation in computer science.

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: Sponsored by Microsoft, Facebook, Amazon, Google, and other tech companies, Code.org aims to provide opportunities for K-12 students to learn about AI and machine-learning systems [20].

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: To support students (grades 3–12) in learning about AI, algorithms, machine learning, and bias, the organization offers an activity called “AI for Oceans”, where students are able to train their machine-learning models.

verb_relations.subject: bias
verb_relations.sentence: The activity is provided as an open-access tutorial for teachers to help their students explore how to train, model and classify data, as well as to understand how human bias plays a role in machine-learning systems.

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: During the activity, students first classify the objects as either “fish” or “not fish” in an attempt to remove trash from the ocean.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: Then, they expand their training data set by including other sea creatures that belong underwater.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: With the support of their teachers, they discuss machine learning, steps and influences of training data, as well as the formation and risks of biased data [20].

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: In this paper, we provided an overview of the possibilities and potential ethical and societal risks of AI integration in education.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: To help address these risks, we highlighted several instructional strategies and resources for practitioners seeking to integrate AI applications in K-12 education and/or instruct students about the ethical issues they pose.

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: These instructional materials have the potential to help students and teachers reap the powerful benefits of AI while navigating ethical challenges especially related to privacy concerns and bias.

verb_relations.subject: research
verb_relations.sentence: Existing research on AI in education provides insight on supporting students’ understanding and use of AI [2, 13]; however, research on how to develop K-12 teachers’ instructional practices regarding AI and ethics is still in its infancy.

verb_relations.subject: resources
verb_relations.sentence: Moreover, current resources, as demonstrated above, mainly address privacy and bias-related ethical and societal concerns of AI.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: In addition, curriculum developers and workshop designers might consider centering culturally relevant and responsive pedagogies (by focusing on students’ funds of knowledge, family background, and cultural experiences) while creating instructional materials that address surveillance, privacy, autonomy, and bias.

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: In such student-centered learning environments, students voice their own cultural and contextual experiences while trying to critique and disrupt existing power structures and cultivate their social awareness [24, 36].

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: Finally, as scholars in teacher education and educational technology, we believe that educating future generations of diverse citizens to participate in the ethical use and development of AI will require more professional development for K-12 teachers (both pre-service and in-service).

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: For instance, through sustained professional learning sessions, teachers could engage with suggested curriculum resources and teaching strategies as well as build a community of practice where they can share and critically reflect on their experiences with other teachers.

verb_relations.subject: research
verb_relations.sentence: Further research on such reflective teaching practices and students’ sense-making processes in relation to AI and ethics lessons will be essential to developing curriculum materials and pedagogies relevant to a broad base of educators and students.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

relations.sentence: A 14th century illustration by Laurentius de Voltolina depicts a university lecture in medieval Italy.
A 14th century illustration by Laurentius de Voltolina depicts a university lecture in medieval Italy.
Classrooms today do not look much different, though you might find modern students looking at their laptops, tablets, or smart phones instead of books (though probably open to Facebook).

Today, massive amounts of information (books, audio, images, videos) are available at one’s fingertips through the Internet, and opportunities for formal learning are available online worldwide through the Khan Academy, MOOCs, podcasts, traditional online degree programs, and more.

relations.source: an Online Master of Science in Education in Learning Design and Technology
relations.sentence: You can help shape the influence of technology in education with an Online Master of Science in Education in Learning Design and Technology from Purdue University Online.
Learn more about the online MSEd in Learning Design and Technology at Purdue University today and help redefine the way in which individuals learn.

verb_relations.subject: Technology
verb_relations.sentence: How Has Technology Changed Education?

verb_relations.subject: education
verb_relations.sentence: Technology has impacted almost every aspect of life today, and education is no exception.

verb_relations.subject: illustration
verb_relations.sentence: A 14th century illustration by Laurentius de Voltolina depicts a university lecture in medieval Italy.

verb_relations.subject: Some
verb_relations.sentence: Some of the students have books open in front of them and appear to be following along.

verb_relations.subject: technology
verb_relations.sentence: A cynic would say that technology has done nothing to change education.

verb_relations.subject: technology
verb_relations.sentence: However, in many ways, technology has profoundly changed education.

verb_relations.subject: technology
verb_relations.sentence: For one, technology has greatly expanded access to education.

verb_relations.subject: few
verb_relations.sentence: In medieval times, books were rare and only an elite few had access to educational opportunities.

verb_relations.subject: Individuals
verb_relations.sentence: Individuals had to travel to centers of learning to get an education.

verb_relations.subject: technology
verb_relations.sentence: Today, technology enables forms of communication and collaboration undreamt of in the past.

verb_relations.subject: Students
verb_relations.sentence: Students in a classroom in the rural U.S., for example, can learn about the Arctic by following the expedition of a team of scientists in the region, read scientists’ blog posting, view photos, e-mail questions to the scientists, and even talk live with the scientists via a videoconference.

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: Students can share what they are learning with students in other classrooms in other states who are tracking the same expedition.

verb_relations.subject: Students
verb_relations.sentence: Students can collaborate on group projects using technology-based tools such as wikis and Google docs.

verb_relations.subject: technology
verb_relations.sentence: The walls of the classrooms are no longer a barrier as technology enables new ways of learning, communicating, and working collaboratively.

verb_relations.subject: Technology
verb_relations.sentence: Technology has also begun to change the roles of teachers and learners.

verb_relations.subject: learners
verb_relations.sentence: In the traditional classroom, such as what we see depicted in de Voltolina’s illustration, the teacher is the primary source of information, and the learners passively receive it.

verb_relations.subject: students
verb_relations.sentence: However, because of the access to information and educational opportunity that technology has enabled, in many classrooms today we see the teacher’s role shifting to the “guide on the side” as students take more responsibility for their own learning using technology to gather relevant information.

verb_relations.subject: Schools
verb_relations.sentence: Schools and universities across the country are beginning to redesign learning spaces to enable this new model of education, foster more interaction and small group work, and use technology as an enabler.

verb_relations.subject: people
verb_relations.sentence: Technology is a powerful tool that can support and transform education in many ways, from making it easier for teachers to create instructional materials to enabling new ways for people to learn and work together.

verb_relations.subject: education
verb_relations.sentence: It will be up to instructional designers and educational technologies to make the most of the opportunities provided by technology to change education so that effective and efficient education is available to everyone everywhere.

verb_relations.subject: You
verb_relations.sentence: You can help shape the influence of technology in education with an Online Master of Science in Education in Learning Design and Technology from Purdue University Online.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: This accredited program offers studies in exciting new technologies that are shaping education and offers students the opportunity to take part in the future of innovation.

verb_relations.subject: individuals
verb_relations.sentence: Learn more about the online MSEd in Learning Design and Technology at Purdue University today and help redefine the way in which individuals learn.

relations.sentence: When the Stanford Accelerator for Learning and the Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI began planning the inaugural AI+Education Summit last year, the public furor around AI had not reached its current level.
“Technology offers the prospect of universal access to increase fundamentally new ways of teaching,” said Graduate School of Education Dean Daniel Schwartz in his opening remarks.

Researchers across Stanford – from education, technology, psychology, business, law, and political science – joined industry leaders like Sal Khan, founder and CEO of Khan Academy, in sharing cutting-edge research and brainstorming ways to unlock the potential of AI in education in an ethical, equitable, and safe manner.

Enabling learning without fear of judgment

verb_relations.subject: AI
verb_relations.sentence: AI Will Transform Teaching and Learning.

verb_relations.subject: researchers
verb_relations.sentence: At the recent AI+Education Summit, Stanford researchers, students, and industry leaders discussed both the potential of AI to transform education for the better and the risks at play.

verb_relations.subject: furor
verb_relations.sentence: When the Stanford Accelerator for Learning and the Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI began planning the inaugural AI+Education Summit last year, the public furor around AI had not reached its current level.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: Even so, intensive research was already underway across Stanford University to understand the vast potential of AI, including generative AI, to transform education as we know it.

verb_relations.subject: it
verb_relations.sentence: By the time the summit was held on Feb. 15, ChatGPT had reached more than 100 million unique users, and 30% of all college students had used it for assignments, making it one of the fastest-ever applications ever adopted overall – and certainly in education settings.

verb_relations.subject: Summit
verb_relations.sentence: The AI+Education Summit explored a central question: How can AI like this and other applications be best used to advance human learning?

verb_relations.subject: Schwartz
verb_relations.sentence: “Technology offers the prospect of universal access to increase fundamentally new ways of teaching,” said Graduate School of Education Dean Daniel Schwartz in his opening remarks.

verb_relations.subject: lot
verb_relations.sentence: “I want to emphasize that a lot of AI is also going to automate really bad ways of teaching.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: So [we need to] think about it as a way of creating new types of teaching.”

verb_relations.subject: Researchers
verb_relations.sentence: Researchers across Stanford – from education, technology, psychology, business, law, and political science – joined industry leaders like Sal Khan, founder and CEO of Khan Academy, in sharing cutting-edge research and brainstorming ways to unlock the potential of AI in education in an ethical, equitable, and safe manner.

verb_relations.subject: faculty
verb_relations.sentence: Participants also spent a major portion of the day engaged in small discussion groups in which faculty, students, researchers, staff, and other guests shared their ideas about AI in education.

verb_relations.subject: teachers
verb_relations.sentence:
Discussion topics included natural language processing applied to education; developing students’ AI literacy; assisting students with learning differences; informal learning outside of school; fostering creativity; equity and closing achievement gaps; workforce development; and avoiding potential misuses of AI with students and teachers.
Several themes emerged over the course of the day on AI’s potential, as well as its significant risks.
First, a look at AI’s potential:
1. Enhancing personalized support for teachers at scale
Great teachers remain the cornerstone of effective learning.

verb_relations.subject: teachers
verb_relations.sentence: Yet teachers receive limited actionable feedback to improve their practice.

verb_relations.subject: models
verb_relations.sentence:
AI presents an opportunity to support teachers as they refine their craft at scale through applications such as:
Simulating students: AI language models can serve as practice students for new teachers.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: Percy Liang, director of the Stanford HAI Center for Research on Foundation Models, said that they are increasingly effective and are now capable of demonstrating confusion and asking adaptive follow-up questions.

verb_relations.subject: AI
verb_relations.sentence: Dora Demszky, assistant professor of education data science, highlighted the ability for AI to provide real-time feedback and suggestions to teachers (e.g., questions to ask the class), creating a bank of live advice based on expert pedagogy.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: Demszky added that AI can produce post-lesson reports that summarize the classroom dynamics.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: Potential metrics include student speaking time or identification of the questions that triggered the most engagement.

verb_relations.subject: teachers
verb_relations.sentence: Sal Khan, founder of online learning environment Khan Academy, suggested that AI could help teachers stay up-to-date with the latest advancements in their field.

verb_relations.subject: AI
verb_relations.sentence: For example, a biology teacher would have AI update them on the latest breakthroughs in cancer research, or leverage AI to update their curriculum.

verb_relations.subject: it
verb_relations.sentence:
Changing what is important for learners
Stanford political science Professor Rob Reich proposed a compelling question: Is generative AI comparable to the calculator in the classroom, or will it be a more detrimental tool?

verb_relations.subject: calculator
verb_relations.sentence: Today, the calculator is ubiquitous in middle and high schools, enabling students to quickly solve complex computations, graph equations, and solve problems.

verb_relations.subject: Students
verb_relations.sentence: However, it has not resulted in the removal of basic mathematical computation from the curriculum: Students still know how to do long division and calculate exponents without technological assistance.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: However, they no longer need to be fully proficient – in other words, doing all computation by hand or writing all essays without AI support.

verb_relations.subject: AI
verb_relations.sentence: In fact, by no longer requiring mastery of proficiency, Demszky argued that AI may actually raise the bar.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: The models won’t be doing the thinking for the students; rather, students will now have to edit and curate, forcing them to engage deeper than they have previously.

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: In Khan’s view, this allows learners to become architects who are able to pursue something more creative and ambitious.

verb_relations.subject: which
verb_relations.sentence:
And Noah Goodman, associate professor of psychology and of computer science, questioned the analogy, saying this tool may be more like the printing press, which led to democratization of knowledge and did not eliminate the need for human writing skills.
3.

verb_relations.subject: AI
verb_relations.sentence:
Enabling learning without fear of judgment
Ran Liu, chief AI scientist at Amira Learning, said that AI has the potential to support learners’ self-confidence.

verb_relations.subject: Teachers
verb_relations.sentence: Teachers commonly encourage class participation by insisting that there is no such thing as a stupid question.

verb_relations.subject: fear
verb_relations.sentence: However, for most students, fear of judgment from their peers holds them back from fully engaging in many contexts.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: Interfaces that leverage AI can offer constructive feedback that does not carry the same stakes or cause the same self-consciousness as a human’s response.

verb_relations.subject: Learners
verb_relations.sentence: Learners are therefore more willing to engage, take risks, and be vulnerable.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: With AI, a real-time agent can provide support and feedback, and learners are able to try different tactics as they seek to improve.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: Bryan Brown, professor of education, said that “what we know about learning is not reflected in how we teach.”

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: AI has the potential to support a single teacher who is trying to generate 35 unique conversations with each student.

verb_relations.subject: inability
verb_relations.sentence: During a roundtable discussion facilitated by Stanford Digital Economy Lab Director Erik Brynjolfsson and Candace Thille, associate professor of education and faculty lead on adult learning at the Stanford Accelerator for Learning, attendees noted that the inability to judge a learner’s skill profile is a leading industry challenge.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: AI has the potential to quickly determine a learner’s skills, recommend solutions to fill the gaps, and match them with roles that actually require those skills.

verb_relations.subject: output
verb_relations.sentence:
Now a look at AI’s significant risks:
1. Model output does not reflect true cultural diversity

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: At present, ChatGPT and AI more broadly generates text in language that fails to reflect the diversity of students served by the education system or capture the authentic voice of diverse populations.

verb_relations.subject: ChatGPT
verb_relations.sentence: When the bot was asked to speak in the cadence of the author of The Hate U Give, which features an African American protagonist, ChatGPT simply added “yo” in front of random sentences.

verb_relations.subject: gap
verb_relations.sentence: As Sarah Levine, assistant professor of education, explained, this overwhelming gap fails to foster an equitable environment of connection and safety for some of America’s most underserved learners.

verb_relations.subject: ChatGPT
verb_relations.sentence: While ChatGPT spits out answers to queries, these responses are not designed to optimize for student learning.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: As Liang noted, the models are trained to deliver answers as fast as possible, but that is often in conflict with what would be pedagogically sound, whether that’s a more in-depth explanation of key concepts or a framing that is more likely to spark curiosity to learn more.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence:
Incorrect responses come in pretty packages
Goodman demonstrated that AI can produce coherent text that is completely erroneous.

verb_relations.subject: lab
verb_relations.sentence: His lab trained a virtual tutor that was tasked with solving and explaining algebra equations in a chatbot format.

verb_relations.subject: that
verb_relations.sentence: The chatbot would produce perfect sentences that exhibited top-quality teaching techniques, such as positive reinforcement, but fail to get to the right mathematical answer.

verb_relations.subject: Advances
verb_relations.sentence: Advances exacerbate a motivation crisis

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: Chris Piech, assistant professor of computer science, told a story about a student who recently came into his office crying.

verb_relations.subject: this
verb_relations.sentence: The student was concerned about the rapid progress of ChatGPT and how this would deter future job prospects after many years of learning how to code.

verb_relations.subject: they
verb_relations.sentence: Piech connected the incident to a broader existential motivation crisis, where many students may no longer know what they should be focusing on or don’t see the value of their hard-earned skills.

verb_relations.subject: mission
verb_relations.sentence:
The full impact of AI in education remains unclear at this juncture, but as all speakers agreed, things are changing, and now is the time to get it right.
Stanford HAI’s mission is to advance AI research, education, policy and practice to improve the human condition. Learn more

relations.sentence: Mean overall survival was 8.9 months [median 8 months, (IC95%, 3.9 - 15.5)].
Differences between first line and R/R settings were observed, since average overall survival in first line was 12.0 months, duplicating the OS in R/R which was 7.3 months.

3 Results
3.1 Selection of Studies Search Results
A total of 1,245 publications were identified within the PubMed search and were assessed with the help of ASReview to find relevant publications.
Despite the small number of studies included (13 trials), the average of overall survival was 8.9 months, with a median of 8.0 months (95%CI: 3.9 - 15.5 months).
Despite the small number of studies included (13 trials), the average of overall survival was 8.9 months, with a median of 8.0 months (95%CI: 3.9 - 15.5 months).

Differences between first line and R/R settings were observed, since the average of overall survival in first line was (n = 4) 12.0 months, with a median of 13.1 months (95%CI: 4.7 - 17.7 months), duplicating the OS in R/R of 7.3 months (mean), and median of 6.7 months (95%CI: 4.5 - 10.7 months).
Despite the lack of published evidence about this variable, we noted a greater overall survival in the studies in which patients were treated in first line with immunotherapy than in those in which treated patients were in relapse or were refractory to a first line.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: In this study, we review the available evidence on the use of ICI, either in monotherapy or in combination with other treatments, in different AML settings, including newly diagnosed (ND) AML, relapsed or refractory (R/R) AML and maintenance treatment after allogeneic-HSCT (allo-HSCT), among others.

verb_relations.subject: who
verb_relations.sentence: The primary objective of this systematic review and meta-analysis is to assess the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitor-based immunotherapy (ICI) in acute myeloid leukemia (AML), either after first-line chemotherapy with allo-HSCT, in refractory or relapsed disease and in patients who are not candidates for intensive chemotherapy who are treated with hypomethylating agents.

verb_relations.subject: objectives
verb_relations.sentence: Secondary objectives include the assessment of checkpoint inhibitor-mediated immune-mediated toxicities.

verb_relations.subject: keywords
verb_relations.sentence: Particularly, the search keywords used included leukemia, acute myeloid, acute myelogenous, cancer, neoplasm*, malginanc*, hemato*, haemato*, Anti-PD1, Anti-PD-L1, Anti-CTLA-4, Anti-TIM3, Anti-LAG-3, Anti-CD70 and their drugs names (both commercial and development drug names) in clinical trial development.

verb_relations.subject: I2
verb_relations.sentence: The heterogeneity was assessed using I2 values: low (I2 = 0%–25%), medium (I2 = 25%–50%), high (I2 = 50%–75%), and nonignorable (I2 = 75%–100%).

verb_relations.sentence: From those publications, there were 80 abstracts that matched our selection criteria.

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: After removing duplicated studies via Mendeley and screening the titles, abstracts, and full texts of all eligible studies, we used the following standards to select studies for inclusion: (1) clinical studies involving controlled trials with samples >5 cases, case reports, letters, and reviews were excluded; (2) sufficient data on efficacy and adverse events (AEs).

verb_relations.subject: which
verb_relations.sentence: The vast majority of them were early phase trials (including phase I, IB and phase II), which mainly measured these immunotherapies’ toxicity profiles and response rates (11, 14–25).

verb_relations.subject: who

verb_relations.subject: we
verb_relations.sentence: For this reason, we analyzed the overall response rate in each of these scenarios, the results of which are shown in Figure 2 .

verb_relations.subject: others

3.4 Overall Survival (OS)
Due to the lack of consistency in the reporting of this variable across the trials, in this section we have proceeded to calculate the mean and median of these results, without being able to represent the forest plot of these results, due to the limitation of evidence.

Publication bias is a matter of concern in meta-analysis.
